condition.qualitative_description: Per-capita cropland availability below 0.4 ha/person is a proxy for marginal or sub-marginal average farm size, consistent with landholding fragmentation in densely populated semi-arid villages.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Dividing total MWS cropland by village population gives a rough proxy for average farm size per household. In Vidarbha and Marathwada, average operational holdings are well below 1 ha for marginal farmers. A ratio below 0.4 ha/person (roughly 1.6 ha for a household of four) indicates that most households operate sub-economic holdings incapable of generating marketable surplus, directly constraining farm income. Shrinking average landholding size is identified as a pressing structural challenge for Indian agriculture.

condition.qualitative_description: Mean cropping intensity at or below 1.15 indicates predominantly single-season rainfed cultivation with little double-cropping, compounding the income constraint imposed by small landholding size.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Low cropping intensity means small holders are not compensating for limited land area through intensification. In semi-arid hard-rock plateau settings, single kharif cropping dominates due to rainfall unreliability and poor groundwater access. When a household with less than 1.5 ha cultivates only one season, effective annual production is severely limited, leaving households dependent on a single livelihood source with no buffer against crop failure.

condition.qualitative_description: Village is more than 30 km from the nearest APMC and more than 20 km from the nearest dairy collection centre, indicating that small holders face high transaction costs and are unable to diversify into livestock income.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Small holders near APMCs benefit from competitive price discovery and reduced dependence on intermediaries; remote smallholders are forced to sell at farm-gate prices well below MSP. Similarly, proximity to dairy infrastructure is the primary route for livestock income diversification for marginal farmers. When both market access channels are distant, the income constraint from small landholding is amplified: households cannot realise fair crop prices and cannot supplement farm income through milk sales. FPO formation is recommended precisely to overcome this market access barrier for unorganised small farmers.

condition.expression: nrega_community_assets_count < 10 and ('livelihoods' not in organization_domains and 'fisheries' not in organization_domains)
condition.qualitative_description: Fewer than 10 cumulative MGNREGA community asset works and no NGO presence in livelihoods or fisheries domains indicate a weak safety net and absence of diversification support for small holders.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: MGNREGA community asset works provide off-farm wage income that supplements small farm earnings; a low cumulative count signals that this safety net is underdeveloped. Simultaneously, the absence of NGOs working in livelihoods or fisheries means there is no institutional support for income diversification — a critical gap given that households solely dependent on small-scale agriculture have lower monthly incomes and wellbeing than those with diversified livelihoods. FPOs and NGO-led extension are identified as key institutional interfaces for small farmers to access markets and diversification opportunities.

condition.qualitative_description: If local records or field surveys indicate that more than 60% of operational holdings are marginal (<1 ha) or small (1–2 ha), this directly confirms landholding fragmentation as the primary income constraint. This variable is not available in the MWS database and must be elicited from field sources or district agricultural census data.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Direct measurement of landholding size distribution is the most reliable confirmation of this pathway. In Vidarbha and Marathwada, district-level agricultural census data consistently show that over 60–70% of holdings are marginal or small. Without this variable, the diagnosis relies on proxy signals (sig_01–sig_04). If field surveys or block-level census data confirm high proportions of marginal holders, the pathway confidence upgrades from medium to high.

condition.qualitative_description: Mean household or farm income below approximately Rs 50,000/year confirms severe income insufficiency on small holdings; Rs 50,000–1,00,000 partial; above weakens landholding as the primary driver.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Direct income evidence distinguishes volume-poverty (small area) from market or price failure (see market_price_crop amplifier).

condition.qualitative_description: Farm-gate price consistently well below MSP (roughly 20–40% gap) for the main crop, amplifying income loss from small land area.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Market price failure amplifies small_landholding stress; near-MSP prices suggest low volume rather than exploitation is the dominant constraint.

confounders.confounder: Drought or rainfall failure causing low income rather than structural landholding fragmentation
confounders.how_to_distinguish: Check drought_severe_return_period and mean_annual_precipitation_mm. If severe droughts recur every 2–3 years and mean precipitation is below 600 mm, drought is a co-primary driver. Distinguish by asking whether income was adequate in good rainfall years — if yes, drought is primary; if income was always insufficient regardless of rainfall, small landholding is the structural cause. Also check trend_cropping_intensity: a declining trend alongside low mean_cropping_intensity suggests drought suppression of double-cropping rather than structural fragmentation alone.

confounders.confounder: High input costs or crop price collapse causing low income despite adequate landholding size
confounders.how_to_distinguish: If lulc_cropland_ha[-1] / village_total_population > 0.6 (suggesting adequate average farm size) but income remains low, the pathway is more likely input cost squeeze or market price failure than landholding fragmentation. Elicit market_price_crop data to check price realisation vs MSP. If the farm-gate price is consistently below MSP for the dominant crop (cotton, soybean), price failure rather than land size is the binding constraint.

missing_variable_questions.question_to_user: Approximately what proportion of farming households in this village own less than 1 acre (0.4 ha) of land? Would you say it is fewer than one-quarter, about half, or more than three-quarters of households?
missing_variable_questions.how_answer_updates_diagnosis: If more than half of households own less than 1 acre, this directly confirms the small landholding pathway as the primary income constraint and upgrades overall confidence to high. If most households own 2–5 acres but income is still low, the diagnosis should shift toward market access failure or input cost pathways.

choices.label: Less than one-quarter of farming households own under 1 ha
About half of farming households own under 1 ha
More than half (or two-thirds) of households are marginal (<1 ha)

missing_variable_questions.question_to_user: What is the approximate total annual household income (from all sources — crops, livestock, wages, government schemes) for a typical farming family in this village? Is it below Rs 60,000, between Rs 60,000–1,20,000, or above Rs 1,20,000 per year?
missing_variable_questions.how_answer_updates_diagnosis: Annual household income below Rs 60,000 (approximately Rs 5,000/month) confirms the low_income stress observation. The breakdown by source (farm vs off-farm vs transfer) reveals whether diversification is already occurring or absent, directly informing whether FPO formation or non-farm livelihood promotion is the priority intervention.

choices.label: Below ₹50,000 per year from farming (or total household income)

missing_variable_questions.question_to_user: For the main crop grown here (e.g. cotton, soybean, tur dal), what price did farmers typically receive per quintal in the last harvest season? How does this compare to the government's Minimum Support Price (MSP)?
missing_variable_questions.how_answer_updates_diagnosis: If farm-gate prices are consistently 20–40% below MSP, market price failure is amplifying the income constraint from small landholding. This would strengthen the case for FPO-based collective marketing as the primary intervention. If prices are near MSP but income is still low, the constraint is purely on the production volume side (small area × low yield), confirming the landholding fragmentation pathway.

choices.label: Prices close to government MSP (mandi or procurement)
Prices significantly below MSP (e.g. farm-gate or trader)
Unable to sell at all or forced to accept very low prices

sources.title: Revitalizing Indian Agriculture and Boosting Farmer Incomes
sources.relevance_note: Documents shrinking average landholding size as a structural constraint on Indian farmer incomes; establishes agriculture–poverty–nutrition linkage and the need for income augmentation interventions in semi-arid states.

sources.title: Farmer producer organisations: Innovative institutions for upliftment of small farmers
sources.relevance_note: Documents how FPOs provide market access, bargaining power, and economy of scale for unorganised small farmers; identifies marketing benefits and the role of linkages as critical for small holder income improvement.

sources.title: Enhancing farmers income through farmers' producers companies in India: Status and roadmap
sources.relevance_note: Reviews FPC status and growth in India; documents how farmer-owned micro-enterprises bridge the gap between small fragmented holdings and market access, directly addressing the income constraint from landholding fragmentation.

sources.title: The interplay between household food security and wellbeing among small-scale farmers in the context of rapid agrarian change
sources.relevance_note: Empirically demonstrates that households solely dependent on small-scale agriculture have lower monthly incomes and wellbeing than those with diversified livelihoods; supports the diagnosis that single-source livelihood dependence amplifies income stress for small holders.

sources.title: The Role of Smallholder Farms in a Changing World
sources.relevance_note: Provides typology of smallholder constraints distinguishing soft constraints (market access, credit, infrastructure) from hard constraints (high population density, low rainfall, remote location); directly applicable to Deccan Plateau semi-arid context and informs move-up vs move-out strategy recommendations.

overall_reasoning_note: Confirm this pathway when at least two of sig_01 (low per-capita cropland), sig_02 (low cropping intensity), and sig_03 (poor market access) are triggered simultaneously. sig_04 (weak safety net and absent diversification support) amplifies but does not independently confirm. sig_05 (qualitative landholding distribution) is the gold-standard confirmation when field data are available. Distinguish from drought-driven income stress by checking whether income was adequate in good rainfall years. Distinguish from price-failure pathways by checking whether farm size is adequate but price realisation is below MSP. In Vidarbha and Marathwada, both landholding fragmentation and cotton price failure frequently co-occur — treat as compound stress requiring both FPO formation and crop diversification interventions.
